interactions.summary: Jiang says Freud still needed a technique to make patients accept the reversal from believed trauma to sexual fantasy; dream interpretation becomes the method because dreams let the analyst suggest new ideas and recode memory.

He moves from Freud and Jung to modernism's spread through art, using Joyce's Ulysses as the first great modernist case: musical, allusive, elitist, self-referential, and different from Homer or Dante's democratic truth-seeking.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture with quoted Joyce passage.

interactions.summary: Jiang reads Virginia Woolf as a Freud-influenced stream-of-consciousness writer concerned with memory and perception, then contrasts modern literature's inward self-salvation with Dostoevsky's view that psychology responds to the world and redemption requires others.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture with quoted Woolf passage.

speaker_notes.note: Joyce and Woolf passages are read aloud by Jiang. Treat them as quoted material embedded in lecture, not separate present speakers.

claims.claim: Jiang presents dream interpretation as Freud's solution for persuading patients to reinterpret remembered abuse as fantasy, because dreams allow subtle suggestion and memory recoding.
claims.temporal_scope: Freud method critique in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

claims.claim: Jung helped Freud's influence spread by systematizing the unconscious into popular dualities such as ego/shadow, conscious/unconscious, personal/collective, and anima/animus.
Jiang identifies James Joyce's Ulysses as an early great modernist work that imitates and tries to surpass Dante while also invoking Homer's Odyssey.
claims.temporal_scope: Literary history claim in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

claims.claim: Joyce's difficulty, in Jiang's reading, comes from musical style and dense allusion: readers must have read and experienced what Joyce did in order to understand him.
Jiang contrasts Joyce with Dante by saying Joyce demands that readers access Joyce's own mind of God, whereas Dante uses poetry to bring people toward the mind of God as truth of the world.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative literary model in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

claims.claim: Modern literature, represented by Ulysses, is described as elitist, self-referential, allusive, stream-of-consciousness writing that breaks from older literature's effort to empower people to seek truth.
Woolf's To the Lighthouse is presented as Freud-influenced stream of consciousness about memory, perception, and remembering.
claims.temporal_scope: Literary history claim in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang contrasts Dostoevsky with modern literature: Dostoevsky's psychology responds to external events and requires surrender to others, while modern literature promises self-discovery, self-mastery, and self-redemption.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative literary model in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Dreams become useful because they are soft enough for the analyst to implant a new story.
signature_moments.source_phrase: to basically implant new memories and basically gaslight that person
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's harshest explanation of psychoanalytic method: not discovery of truth, but guided replacement of memory.

signature_moments.moment: Dante brings ordinary readers toward the mind of God; Joyce makes readers spend decades trying to enter Joyce's own mind.
signature_moments.source_phrase: James Joyce believes that he is God
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This captures Jiang's indictment of modernist art as a religious reversal from shared truth to private genius.

signature_moments.moment: Dostoevsky's heart is a deep ocean that needs other people; modern literature turns inward and tries to save itself.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the heart, it's a deep impenetrable ocean
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This contrast prepares the final social argument against the cult of the self.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Freud's dream-analysis method, interpreted by Jiang as a way to redirect patients away from contested memories and toward suggested fantasy explanations.
Modernist writing that tries to capture the mind as it thinks and works.
Jiang's category for inward, elitist, self-referential literature organized around self-discovery and self-mastery.

chronology_notes.note: This packet moves from Freud's method of dream interpretation to Jung's popularization and modernist literary forms, setting up the final claim that modernism spreads the cult of the self socially and politically.

uncertainty_notes.note: The Joyce quotation is heavily ASR-distorted; the semantic pass records Jiang's framing of its incomprehensibility rather than the exact literary text.
Jiang appears to say Virginia Woolf published or was close to Joyce; the packet preserves only the relevant transition into Woolf, not the exact publication claim.
